skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render React Remotion TSX into MP4 videos through inference.sh with configurable duration, resolution, FPS, codec, props, and Remotion animation APIs.
skill.summary: Render Remotion React components into MP4 videos through inference.sh.
skill.author: inference-sh-8

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified a pipe-to-shell installer pattern and real network use, but most Ruby backtick and weak-cryptography alerts are Markdown false positives. The skill appears legitimate and purpose-built for remote Remotion rendering, yet it should publish with warnings about executing installer scripts, sending TSX and props to inference.sh, and the broad infsh CLI allowance.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-to-shell CLI installation instruction
medium_findings.description: The static pipe-to-shell finding at SKILL.md line 17 is a true positive for a dangerous install pattern. It fetches a remote shell script and executes it immediately, which creates supply-chain risk even though line 29 documents checksum verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command directly pipes a remote URL into sh. The surrounding install note suggests legitimate intent, so this is risky but not confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Broad inference.sh CLI command allowance
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash(infsh *), which permits any inference.sh CLI subcommand rather than only the remotion-render app. This is broader than required and can run other remote app operations under the user account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowlist is explicit and semantically broader than the documented render workflow. Legitimate use is likely, but the scope should be narrowed.

medium_findings.title: Remote rendering receives user TSX and props
medium_findings.description: The render workflow sends user-provided TSX code and optional props to inference.sh for processing. This is the intended function, but users may expose proprietary code, media references, or sensitive data in props.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples and input schema show code and props sent to a named remote app. This is expected behavior, but it is a clear privacy boundary.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded external URLs are expected service links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are true positives for network references, but they point to the documented service, image asset, and official docs. No credential exfiltration or hidden callback endpoint was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation and asset links. I did not find evidence that secrets are sent to an unrelated endpoint.

low_findings.title: Markdown syntax caused static-analysis false positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution findings are false positives caused by Markdown fences, inline code, and example commands. The weak cryptography findings at lines 3 and 33 were not confirmed as real cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are frontmatter text, Markdown code fences, a table header, or import examples. There is no Ruby execution or weak hash call in those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe-to-shell CLI installation instruction
dangerous_patterns.description: The static pipe-to-shell finding at SKILL.md line 17 is a true positive for a dangerous install pattern. It fetches a remote shell script and executes it immediately, which creates supply-chain risk even though line 29 documents checksum verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command directly pipes a remote URL into sh. The surrounding install note suggests legitimate intent, so this is risky but not confirmed malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad inference.sh CLI command allowance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows Bash(infsh *), which permits any inference.sh CLI subcommand rather than only the remotion-render app. This is broader than required and can run other remote app operations under the user account.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowlist is explicit and semantically broader than the documented render workflow. Legitimate use is likely, but the scope should be narrowed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote rendering receives user TSX and props
dangerous_patterns.description: The render workflow sends user-provided TSX code and optional props to inference.sh for processing. This is the intended function, but users may expose proprietary code, media references, or sensitive data in props.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples and input schema show code and props sent to a named remote app. This is expected behavior, but it is a clear privacy boundary.

content.value_statement: Creating videos from React code requires a repeatable rendering pipeline. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code send Remotion TSX to inference.sh and return MP4 output.
actual_capabilities: Renders a default-exported Remotion React component into an MP4 video.
Accepts width, height, FPS, duration, codec, composition ID, and props.
Supports common Remotion APIs including AbsoluteFill, Sequence, spring, and interpolate.
Provides CLI examples for fade-in text, counters, spring motion, props, and sequences.
Shows Python SDK usage for batch rendering and streaming progress updates.
limitations: Requires an inference.sh account, CLI setup, or SDK access.
Sends submitted TSX code and props to an external rendering service.
Only documents React and Remotion imports as supported rendering dependencies.
Does not review submitted TSX for privacy, licensing, or runtime safety.

use_cases.title: Generate branded motion graphics
use_cases.description: Create short MP4 clips from reusable Remotion components with controlled timing and resolution.

use_cases.title: Automate data-driven videos
use_cases.description: Render charts, counters, status summaries, or personalized messages by passing props into a component.

use_cases.title: Prototype React animation exports
use_cases.description: Test Remotion animation ideas quickly and export playable videos for review.

prompt_templates.title: Render a basic animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple MP4 render.
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a three-second Remotion video with centered fade-in title text. Use 1920 by 1080 resolution and 30 FPS.

prompt_templates.title: Create a social clip
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request with format and style constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a vertical Remotion video for a product announcement. Use animated headline text, a color background, and five seconds duration.

prompt_templates.title: Render with props
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for reusable component data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a Remotion component that receives title and subtitle props. Use the props to produce a short branded intro video.

prompt_templates.title: Build a sequenced motion system
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request using multiple Remotion timing primitives.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Remotion video with three timed scenes, spring transitions, and progress-based opacity changes. Render it as an MP4.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a three-second fade-in title animation at 1080p.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a Remotion component, sends it to inference.sh, and returns the MP4 render location.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a square animated loading counter with 60 FPS playback.
output_examples.output: The assistant renders a five-second square video with frame-based counter movement and reports the output video link.

output_examples.input: A user asks to render a reusable intro with custom title and subtitle values.
output_examples.output: The assistant passes the requested props to the component and returns a rendered intro video.

best_practices: Review TSX and props for secrets before sending them to inference.sh.
Pin duration, FPS, width, and height before rendering to avoid unexpected output.
Prefer manual CLI installation and checksum verification over pipe-to-shell installation.
anti_patterns: Do not include private credentials, unreleased code, or sensitive customer data in props.
Do not run installer commands without checking the source and checksum first.
Do not assume every npm package or browser API is available inside the renderer.

faq.question: What does this skill render?
faq.answer: It renders React Remotion component code into MP4 videos through the inference.sh remotion-render app.

faq.question: Does it run locally?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow sends render jobs to inference.sh through the CLI or Python SDK.

faq.question: Can I pass dynamic data?
faq.answer: Yes. The input schema supports props, which are passed into the default exported component.

faq.question: Which Remotion APIs are documented?
faq.answer: The skill documents useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence, Audio, Video, and Img.

faq.question: What security warning should users know?
faq.answer: Users should avoid sending secrets or private source code and should verify any CLI installer before running it.

faq.question: Is this skill blocked from publishing?
faq.answer: No. The audit found medium risk from expected remote rendering and installer guidance, but no confirmed malicious intent.
